description: To speak what one knows to be false with the intent to mislead. 'Peshu' covers spoken and enacted deception—false words, false silence, truth withheld to create a wrong belief. It contrasts with 'phelira' (mistaken): the mistaken speaker is wrong without knowing it, while the liar knows and chooses. Phi keeps this word not to enable deception but to let speakers name it plainly.
sound_symbolism: The stop 'p' erects a hard false front — the surface presented to the listener. Behind it, 'shu' is the whisper closing over the closed vowel 'u': the truth pushed down into concealment. The word shares no sounds with 'theloa' (truth); the two cannot be mistaken for each other.
grammatical_notes: Functions as an intransitive verb of speech: 'shia to peshu.' (They lied.) Negation 'ma' comes immediately before the verb: 'mia ma peshu.' (I do not lie.) Prohibition: 'no ma peshu.' (Do not lie.) Reported content takes the 'mena … meno' frame. Contrast 'phelira' (mistaken), which carries no intent, and 'wero' (honest) and 'theloa' (truth), its opposites.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Right speech begins with abstaining from false speech—a precept speakers can only keep if the language lets them name its breach. 'Peshu' names the act without euphemism.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Peaceful speech does not mean avoiding hard words for wrong acts. A plain word for lying lets communities address deception directly and repair trust, instead of letting it hide behind softer terms.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Oral cultures ran on the reliability of the spoken word—agreements, boundaries, and histories lived in speech alone, so the liar endangered the community's shared memory. The word carried real weight.

tags.dialogue: False speech; the breaking of communicative trust
